summary: Gruber chronicles the chaotic collapse of Twitter's workforce under Elon Musk's leadership, focusing on the mass resignations following Musk's 'extremely hardcore' ultimatum. After already laying off half the company, Musk's loyalty-test deadline triggered hundreds more departures, including entire critical engineering teams. Gruber highlights the absurdity of Musk's demand for code screenshots as a measure of programmer value, and quotes departing engineers who left not out of hatred for Musk but because no vision was ever shared. Gruber admits his earlier optimism about Musk's Twitter was wrongheaded, noting that while bad product decisions can be reversed, destroyed morale and lost talent cannot. He concludes that Musk's every action seems designed to sink his own $44 billion acquisition.
key_points: Musk's 'extremely hardcore' ultimatum triggered hundreds of resignations on top of the prior 50% layoff, leaving Twitter with a skeleton crew and entire critical teams gone

Demanding code commit summaries and screenshots as a programmer evaluation metric reveals a fundamental misunderstanding of software engineering — elite programmers often reduce code, and many essential roles don't involve commits at all
Gruber's earlier optimism was based on the idea that bad product decisions are reversible, but talent loss and destroyed morale are not
Departing engineers like Peter Clowes didn't leave out of hatred for Musk but because no vision was shared — the ultimatum was a pure loyalty test requiring blind faith
The highest priority after layoffs should be restoring morale, yet every Musk action has systematically destroyed it
money_quotes: It's as though Musk has taken Facebook's 'Move fast and break things' motto and reduced it to 'Break everything fast.'
Twitter itself seemed to be documenting its own demise, like HAL 9000 singing 'Daisy', ever more degenerately slurred, near the end of 2001: A Space Odyssey.
Some of the most elite programmers I've ever known have an uncanny knack for reducing lines of code.
Losing essential talent and destroying employee morale, not so much.
There was no vision shared with us. No 5 year plan like at Tesla. Nothing more than what anyone can see on Twitter.
Twitter's marketplace is the most competitive in the world: attention.
He's shooting holes into his own sinking ship.
I do not think he's secretly trying to destroy his own $44 billion acquisition, but if he were, as though in a real-life Brewster's Millions scenario, this path seems like the surest way.
key_insight: Bad product decisions are reversible, but systematically destroying morale and driving out essential talent is a wound a company cannot easily heal.
